label: Yehezkel (Ezekiel) — Part 4 — Chapters 25–32
summary_plain: YHWH delivers oracles against the nations: Ammon, Moav, Edom, the Pelishtim. Then Tyre — the great maritime trading city. The prince of Tyre says: I am a god, I sit in the seat of gods in the heart of the seas. YHWH responds: You are a man and not a god. A lamentation over the king of Tyre: You were in Eden, the garden of The Creator. You were the anointed guardian kerub. You were blameless from the day you were created until unrighteousness was found in you. Against Mitsrayim: Pharaoh the great dragon lying in his rivers. YHWH will scatter Mitsrayim among the nations. The Assyrian was a cedar in Lebanon — great and tall — yet he was brought down to Sheol. Pharaoh shall be laid among the uncircumcised in the pit.
summary_scholarly: The ACR reads from the DSS Ezekiel fragments and the Orit Ge'ez. The Tyre oracles (chapters 26-28) are among the most significant in the prophetic literature. Chapter 28:12-19 — the lamentation over the king of Tyre — describes a figure in Eden, adorned with precious stones, the anointed guardian kerub who walked among the stones of fire until violence was found in him. This passage, alongside Yesha'yahu 14, forms the primary Hebrew prophetic material later systematized into the 'fall of Satan' narrative — though the original text describes the king of Tyre, not a supernatural being. The Mitsrayim oracles (chapters 29-32) include the cedar of Lebanon parable (chapter 31) and the descent to Sheol (chapter 32) — an underworld geography describing where fallen powers are laid.

key_terms.definition: The ruler of the maritime trading city of Tyre who declares: 'I am a god, I sit in the seat of gods in the heart of the seas.' YHWH responds: you are a man and not a god. You shall die the death of the uncircumcised (28:2-10).

In the lamentation over the king of Tyre (28:14): 'You were the anointed guardian kerub — I placed you on the set-apart mountain of The Creator. You walked among the stones of fire.' Later tradition reinterpreted this as the fall of Satan, but the original text addresses the king of Tyre.

The underworld, the pit, the place of the dead. Chapter 32 describes the fallen nations laid in Sheol — Assyria, Elam, Meshekh, Tuval, Edom — all brought down with their weapons of war. Not the later Christian 'hell' but the Hebrew abode of the dead.

Chapter 29:3: Pharaoh is called the great dragon (tannim) lying in the midst of his rivers. YHWH will put hooks in his jaws and pull him out. The dragon imagery connects to ancient Near Eastern chaos-monster traditions.

fill_blank.prompt: Because your heart is proud, and you have said: I am a ______, I sit in the seat of gods, in the heart of the seas.
fill_blank.source_quote: Because your heart is proud, and you have said: I am a god, I sit in the seat of gods, in the heart of the seas — yet you are a man and not a god, though you make your heart like the heart of a god.

fill_blank.prompt: You were blameless in your ways from the day you were ______ until unrighteousness was found in you.
fill_blank.source_quote: You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until unrighteousness was found in you.

fill_blank.prompt: Behold, I am against you, Pharaoh king of Mitsrayim, the great ______ that lies in the midst of his rivers.
fill_blank.source_quote: Behold, I am against you, Pharaoh king of Mitsrayim, the great dragon that lies in the midst of his rivers, who says: My Nile is my own, I made it for myself.

multiple_choice.question: What does the lamentation over the king of Tyre describe?
options: A merchant who lost his wealth
A figure who was in Eden as the anointed guardian kerub until unrighteousness was found in him
A priest who defiled the Temple
multiple_choice.source_quote: You were the signet of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty. You were in Eden, the garden of The Creator. You were the anointed guardian kerub.

multiple_choice.question: Where are the fallen nations described as being laid?
options: In Sheol — the pit — with the uncircumcised
multiple_choice.source_quote: They have laid her among the slain — all of them uncircumcised, slain by the sword. They went down to Sheol with their weapons of war.

multiple_choice.question: What does YHWH declare against Tyre?
options: I will make you a bare rock — a place for spreading nets
I will make you the greatest trading city
I will build you a new harbor
I will make you king over the nations
multiple_choice.source_quote: I will scrape her soil from her and make her a bare rock. She shall be in the midst of the sea a place for the spreading of nets.

faq.question: How did the Great Eraser transform the king of Tyre passage into a 'fall of Satan' narrative?
faq.answer: Yehezkel 28:12-19 addresses the king of Tyre — a human ruler of a maritime trading city. The text describes him as having been in Eden, adorned with precious stones, the anointed guardian kerub who walked among the stones of fire until violence was found in him and he was cast out. The original context is clear: this is a lamentation over a human king whose pride led to his fall. Later Christian interpreters — particularly Origen (3rd century CE) and subsequent Church Fathers — detached this passage from its historical context and combined it with Yesha'yahu 14:12 (the fall of Helel ben Shachar, the morning star — also addressed to a human king, the king of Babylon) to construct the systematic 'fall of Satan' narrative that became standard Christian theology. The Hebrew text addresses human rulers. The Great Eraser repackaged prophetic oracles against specific historical powers into cosmic mythology — removing the political specificity of YHWH's judgment against trading empires and military powers and replacing it with an abstract supernatural drama. The effect: the prophets' confrontation of real political and economic power is spiritualized into harmless mythology.

faq.question: What do the oracles against nations reveal about YHWH's sovereignty over all peoples?
faq.answer: Chapters 25-32 pronounce judgment on seven nations: Ammon, Moav, Edom, the Pelishtim, Tyre, Tsidon, and Mitsrayim. YHWH does not limit his jurisdiction to Yisra'EL — he judges all nations. The principle established in Yovelim 15 applies: every nation is under angelic jurisdiction, but YHWH governs the whole system. Tyre is judged for pride and exploitative trade. Mitsrayim is judged for being a false support — a staff of reed that pierces the hand that leans on it (29:6-7). The oracles establish that YHWH's justice operates internationally. No power — no matter how wealthy (Tyre) or ancient (Mitsrayim) or militarily dominant (Assyria) — stands outside YHWH's judgment. The DSS War Scroll (1QM) extends this framework into the eschatological battle: all nations aligned against the covenant people will face the same judgment Yehezkel pronounces on Tyre and Mitsrayim.
